headlines.title: A TRIBUTE FROM THE MEN WITH WHOM HE TOILED
headlines.subtitle: LeRoy Griswold Shuman
headlines.fullText:
One day a slender lad with bright eyes and almost radiant face came to my den and asked, "May I talk to you a few minutes?" "Certainly, my boy, what is it you have to say:" "The managing editor is going to give me a tryout as cub reporter and I should like to have you give me some rules to word by and to." I answered: "Obey orders, Publishing a daily newspaper In much like fighting a battle on a field that shifts and changes versus The managing editor is the general In the newspaper's field and his reporters are his staff and his orders must be executed just as marshal and saw the boy's wonderful eyes light up until they seemed to Illumine his expressive face in the and went away. I said to myself In the ready parlance of the craft, the managing editor has made a "find." From time to time le inquired of the boy and cast a scrutinizing oye on his handiwork. . It was always good, and it always grew better. That boy was LeRoy Shuman. It Is Incomprehensible that he should have passed away at a time when so much of success lnx ripening to his alert and expanding brain; so much of worthy strife Iny his ready hand: incu der to idly His gift of making friends grew with his other qualities. His true character was just unfolding when the emil came. No mother-nor father-ever had better right to feel proud of a son than Mr. and Mrs. Shuman. No employer ever had a more loyal worker mall The Star-Telegram had In LeRoy Shumaner my Own brother LeRoy. JAMES R. RECORD, News Editor.

It is as a boy-a pure-minded, romping, Jovial boy-I want always to remember him. among his school day friends, he was already falta uns the promise me we had seen In him who Have para mim his home and later in the arrow camps and hikes over the country about Fort Worth Wells, wichita Falls, Waxahachie. His high school days were crowded with accomplishments, too, and he finished The mas popular and leader, and left the high school with a splendid record. He was a real boy in every sense of the word-until he started to work. Then his play was all left behind. No one could do more work than he. Many in the morning I have found him at his desk before most of the force had eaten breakfast. The time never grew too late, not even in the weary hours of a Sunday morning "dog watch." for Lelloy to enthuse over a story." His worthy ambition to idly hhensible that others of us who have been in the grind of the newspaper battle until we wheel into the fighting lite like cavalry bursts a the busle and interpret the order to charge as quick as their masters, Gould not have taken his place un ora death and LeRoy spared to have mus brave fling at the game where the trile of American newspaperdom and the weaklings fall as debris to clutter up But LeRoy Shuman went as he had mewant um through the gathering He was Fun the valley. The night he Segundo the unknown beyond called his father and in tones clear as that must go is out. If will be here soon. I shall obey without fear." man old soldier die once. He had won his stars in the clash and din of the charge, When the final hour came he half rose from a bed of pain and in vibrant tones sald. "All right, general I am awaiting your order." LeRoy Shuman went like a soldier at the call of the Master. A long, sad farewell to that which was mortal of you; a brief good-by to that which is immortal. May a merciful God' be kind to those whom you have left to continue the weary journey. LOUIS J. WORTHAM. Managing Editor.

Pasted on LeRoy Shuman's vacant desk is this quotation clipped from "Concentration Is the motive power of all men who are masters of themselves. Concentration and success Ro That was his unpretentious little motto when phys phere his eye, he didn't clip tout, take it home and use it to decorate his ecran- it squarely in the middle of his workbench. LeRoy Shuman was not an unusual En dictions of his friends of a brilliant future fur him. He was an unusual reporter in that he worked toward a goal and never let such premature applause obscure his respect for the tinjest task. And the truth is-a brilliant future has been blighted. LeRoy he was no more of a plodder than a bright youth who forgot the necessity of en-FTEy. He bristled with energy and he loved his work. If some assignment felt to his lot that was distasteful, he was a master at hu even cheerfulness radiated from him sixty minutes every hour of his long working day. On a newspaper staff, the birds of passage come and go, but LeRoy's place will never be filled. The staff may be as large or It may be larger, but there will never be the same kind of staff an that to which "Little Shu" lent more han Is hin share of camaraderie. to lose a good reporter, but it is a great sorrow to lose a fine, trong young character. LeRoy CHARLES F. PEKOR, JR., City Editor.

LeRoy Shuman has gone from amongst us. "His order." as he expressed It In his last moments, has come and he has answered the call. He bore his long suffering. great as it was, with fortitude that was magnit !- cost to see and faced the inevitable with a calm courage that would have done credit to 'be bravetager. Death held no terrors for Lettet an- man, for he had lived the life of a Christian and his few years upon the Reared rt with free from the tenderers taint care of sin. fond father, he the dawn er pushed a low- a able, beautiful character, equipped in every way to face life's hatties We shall miss LeRoy, we of the editorial department. We shall miss him as a reporter, of course: but, most of all, we shall imiss him as a bright. enthusiastle, lovable boy, a noble little character who came upon life's stage for an all too brief period, but in that time made thore who knew him love him and better for his having lived among them. I have known Leftoy since he was a little boy. romping and playing with the other little boys of the neighbor. He was an alert. bright, sweet little boy then and year by year watched him grow and year by year mis affection for him crew. When take came his into turn the as editorial a "enie desertmunt partant to the strife of a tollsome day in obedience to the orders of the Master and would not be solaced if It were not permitted the rest of the force to add a tribute to the sterling character and steadfast comrade who has fallen in the fight. AMON G. CARTER, BERT N. HONEA. Business Department. W. H. CALKINS Advertising Department. HAROLD V. HOUGH. GUS LUNDBERG, H. M. MAGENDIE, Mailing Department.

headlines.title: Plan for Young Business Men's League Is Commended
headlines.subtitle: Secretary Gets Congratulatory Letters From Other Towns. Speakers Invited for Meeting Wednesday.
headlines.fullText: Business men throughout the city have commended the organization of the Young Men's Business League, according to Ted Robinson, secretary of the new organisation. More than 200 of the 500 promised members already have signed the membership falls and most of these have paid their dues. The work of the organization will be planned at the next regular meeting Wednesday night in the Finney Rifles, armory 7051% Commerce street. The constitution and by-laws will be adopted at that time, It Is meenters at this meeting. Speakers Invited. W.A. Hanger,has been invited to come and he has accepted. Paul Waples and Frank T Crittenden also have been Invited to talks. Secretary Robinson has received letters from the Young Men's Business League of Wars and the Young Men's Hamster Club et Abilene, commanding the movement in Fort Worth, Robinson's reply to the letter from Waco, he asked that city to send an officer er director to Fort Worth Wednesday night. The presence of the Waco man is desired so that he may explain the work of the league in that city. "We are getting new volunteers throughout the city. bare bone thusiastle, ancora Fort Worth, They May we have made asplenild move and are willing to Counts on 15,000. "There is hardly & doubt but that I pour less than two months and we may get it in halt that time." The Young Men's Business League was formally organized at a meeting last Thursday night. The officers are; Dr. Ross Trigg, president; IL. P. Allen Jr .. first vice president; Aaron Gerasbacher, second vice president; J. C. Gantt, third vice president: Ted Robinson, secretary: David Boaz. treasurer, and Fred R. Line, assistant secretary

headlines.title: City Officials Sign Names to Penny Warrant
headlines.fullText: We mind when Fort Worth issued a city warrant for 1 cent Saturday. The cent will be paid to S. Crosbie, workman at the Lake Worth dam. A conimissary is operated at the lake and in drawing warrants for men employed at the dam all commissary charges are first deducted. Crosble's pay, less his commissary charge, left him one cent. The warrant was drawn with the same care and same safeguards that surround the largest warrants Issued by the city. To pay him the 1 cent. Mayor Milam, City Secretary Harwood and City Auditor Martel each had to attach his sharnature to the warrant. bessertomate name of the time of the omnietals required tothetime mately 6 cents after figuring the cost of the paper and printing, and the On its Its terek mere in red ink is the grim that - reward of $100 will be paid for evidence of graft in connection with its issuance.

headlines.title: Elections Coming; Ward Leaguers Are Urged to Wake Up
headlines.fullText: C. A. Wheeler, president of the Sixth Ward Civile League, has called a meeting of the league for Friday night at the Sixth ward fire hall, Peter Smith anton petite President Wheeler in his call says that the club has been Inactive fur several months, but he believes that it is time for it to begin to stir up interest. Elections are apprendmine un President Wheeler beherce men members should get busy in securing men to run for office who will give the people a good business administration. The call, which is also signed by H. M. Durrett, secretario, says there are med every citizen in the Sixth ward to attend the meeting.

headlines.title: Geometry Proves Jeff Surely Will Be Tall as Mutt
headlines.fullText: "When will Jeff be as tall an Mutt?" the question asked of City Street Superintendent Dan Hedge in a mysterious anonymous letter received by him Thursday, has been solvesh The answer is contained in another anonymous letter received Friday. The writeer gives not alone the answer but proceeds to prove it by geometry. It is the height of Jeff's ambition to become a hero of the Mexican war, the waters height of aruatt's ambition to be a hero of the Mexican wammen equal to the same thing are jer each other. The ambition of Jeff and Mutt Is the : mm -Wagn der ne he will therefers at the same time reach Jeff's height, Q. E. D.

headlines.title: Fort Worth Is 'Tres Chic', Says Parisian Miss
headlines.fullText: Miss Madeline Deyber, a slender little French girl, thinks Fort Worth is "tres chic." Friends In New York told her that she would find it quite savage in Texas. She was surprised to find It very nice. Miss Deyber will spend the remainder of the winter with Mr. and Mrs. Andrew Hemphill, 518 Lamar street. Hhe arrived Saturday night at 8:20. after making the journey from Paris alone. She landed In New York last Monday, but spent several days there with friends before starting to Texas.

headlines.title: NEW FIRM FORMED
headlines.subtitle: O. W. Gillespie and Mike Smith Are Partners.
headlines.fullText: O. W. Gillespie, former congressman from this district, and Mike E. Smith, triet court, Saturday formed m partnership to be known as Gillespie and Smith, They will open offices in rooms 1. and 3 in the Dundee building, Seventh and Houston streets.

headlines.title: SOCIETY THE 'TANGO' AND ALL THE LATEST DANCES.
headlines.fullText: Tango, Turkey Trot, Cabaret, Hesitation and all the latest dances will be taught by a Texas girl, Joan Sawyer. and Wallace Mccutcheon, Instructors to the "400" of New York, at the Healy Theater, Monday Tuesday, Feb. 2 and 3. Be sure and get a photo of Maurice Costello at the Healy .- Adv.
